title: Pressed botanical inclusions in handmade paper
subtitle: Pressing dried flowers and leaves between two wet sheets in a single couching
excerpt: Botanical inclusions are objects pressed into the body of a handmade sheet rather than laid on top of it. The technique involves couching a first sheet, placing the dried botanical onto its wet surface, and immediately pulling and couching a second sheet over it. The two sheets merge into one as they dry, trapping the botanical in the fibre structure.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923, public domain), chapter on paper embellishments and inclusions. Internet Archive scan available. The double-couching technique for botanical inclusions is consistent with Hunter's descriptions of inclusion methods in historical papermaking.

glossaryTerms.definition: The action of transferring a freshly pulled wet sheet from the mould onto a damp felt by inverting and pressing the mould downward.
A dried flower, leaf, or plant fragment embedded within the fibre structure of a handmade paper sheet by the double-couching technique.
The technique of placing an inclusion onto a freshly couched wet sheet, then couching a second sheet directly over it so the two sheets bond around the inclusion as they dry.

content.text: A
botanical inclusion
is trapped in the paper rather than pasted to its surface. The
double couch
technique locks the inclusion between two very thin sheets of the same fibre, which hold it without the adhesive or surface treatment that would otherwise alter the sheet's surface.
Keep the blender unplugged when charging with fibre or rinsing. Drain the vat before reaching in with your hands.
What you need

Botanicals: dried and pressed flowers or leaves, pressed flat under weight for at least 1 week before use. Fresh or damp botanicals release moisture and mould during drying. Suitable choices include viola flowers, fern fronds, individual grass stems, and rose petals dried flat.
Cotton linter furnish: very thin, about half the normal concentration. The sheets must be thin enough that when two are pressed together the combined weight is close to a normal single sheet.
The double-couching sequence
Pull a first sheet from the vat using the thin furnish. Couch onto a damp felt in the normal way. The wet sheet should remain on the felt.
Place the pressed botanicals on the wet sheet in the desired composition. Press them gently flat with a fingertip.
Immediately pull a second sheet from the vat at the same consistency. Before
couching
, hold the mould over the sheet and botanicals on the felt.
Couch the second sheet directly over the first. The two wet sheets meet face-to-face with the botanicals sandwiched between them.
Place a fresh damp felt on top of the double sheet. Press in the normal way. During pressing, the two sheets bond and the botanicals are flattened into the combined fibre structure.
Dry under light weight. Avoid drying at high heat, which can cause colour to bleed from botanical petals.
Troubleshooting

attrs.symptom: Botanicals show as bumps after drying rather than as flat inclusions
attrs.cause: The botanical was too thick or not pressed flat before inclusion; the two sheets could not merge fully around it.

content.text: Press botanicals under heavy weight for at least 2 weeks before use to ensure they are as flat as possible. Avoid thick stems and seed heads.

attrs.symptom: Botanicals bleed colour into the surrounding paper
attrs.cause: The botanical was not fully dry; residual moisture released plant dyes into the wet furnish during pressing.

content.text: Dry and press the botanicals for at least 3 weeks in a flower press with silica gel before use. Test first on a small sample sheet.
Variations
Use the same technique to embed thin threads, scraps of fabric, short lengths of coloured yarn, or handmade paper confetti between two sheets. The inclusion does not need to be a botanical.
